1.texto_original: Éstas [son] las palabras de la carta que el profeta Jeremías envió de Jerusalén a los ancianos que habían quedado de los trasportados, y a los sacerdotes y profetas, y a todo el pueblo que Nabucodonosor llevó cautivo de Jerusalén a Babilonia
1.normal:
Devotional Study of Jeremías 29:1

"Éstas [son] las palabras de la carta que el profeta Jeremías envió de Jerusalén a los ancianos que habían quedado de los trasportados, y a los sacerdotes y profetas, y a todo el pueblo que Nabucodonosor llevó cautivo de Jerusalén a Babilonia"

This verse carries deep theological significance. The original Hebrew/Greek text reveals layers of meaning that illuminate Gods character and purposes. The context within the broader narrative provides essential framework for understanding.

The historical backdrop adds richness to interpretation. Archaeological discoveries continue to confirm and illuminate the cultural setting in which these words were written.

For the modern reader, this verse offers both comfort and challenge. It invites us to trust in Gods sovereign plan while actively living out its principles in daily life.
1.academico:
Exegetical Analysis of Jeremías 29:1

Philological Analysis

"Éstas [son] las palabras de la carta que el profeta Jeremías envió de Jerusalén a los ancianos que habían quedado de los trasportados, y a los sacerdotes y profetas, y a todo el pueblo que Nabucodonosor llevó cautivo de Jerusalén a Babilonia"

The original language presents significant lexical and syntactic elements. Key terms reveal theological distinctions often lost in translation.

Historical-Critical Context

Source criticism and form criticism illuminate the compositional history. The Sitz im Leben reflects socio-religious dynamics of antiquity.

Theological Implications

This verse contributes to covenant theology, divine sovereignty, human responsibility, and eschatological hope.

Archaeological Evidence

Recent discoveries from the ancient Near East provide material context for interpreting this passage.

28.normal:
Devotional Study of Jeremías 29:28

"Por tanto, nos envió a decir en Babilonia: Este [cautiverio] es largo; edificad casas, y habitad en ellas; plantad huertos y comed el fruto de ellos."

This verse carries deep theological significance. The original Hebrew/Greek text reveals layers of meaning that illuminate Gods character and purposes. The context within the broader narrative provides essential framework for understanding.

The historical backdrop adds richness to interpretation. Archaeological discoveries continue to confirm and illuminate the cultural setting in which these words were written.

For the modern reader, this verse offers both comfort and challenge. It invites us to trust in Gods sovereign plan while actively living out its principles in daily life.
28.academico:
Exegetical Analysis of Jeremías 29:28

Philological Analysis

"Por tanto, nos envió a decir en Babilonia: Este [cautiverio] es largo; edificad casas, y habitad en ellas; plantad huertos y comed el fruto de ellos."

The original language presents significant lexical and syntactic elements. Key terms reveal theological distinctions often lost in translation.

Historical-Critical Context

Source criticism and form criticism illuminate the compositional history. The Sitz im Leben reflects socio-religious dynamics of antiquity.

Theological Implications

This verse contributes to covenant theology, divine sovereignty, human responsibility, and eschatological hope.

Archaeological Evidence

Recent discoveries from the ancient Near East provide material context for interpreting this passage.
28.ninos:
🌟 Amazing Story! 🌈

Hey kids! 🚀 Today we learn something super cool from the Bible! ✨

"Por tanto, nos envió a decir en Babilonia: Este [cautiverio] es largo; edificad casas, y habitad en ellas; plantad huertos y comed el fruto de ellos."

This verse teaches us something really important about God 💝. He loves us SO much! 🌟

Think about it: just like an artist 🎨 carefully paints every detail, God carefully created everything — including YOU! 😊

Fun Activity: Draw your favorite thing God created! 🖍️🙏❤️

31.texto_original: Envía a decir a todos los de la cautividad: Así dice Jehová de Semaías de Nehelam: Porque os profetizó Semaías, y yo no lo envié, y os hizo confiar en mentira:
31.normal:
Devotional Study of Jeremías 29:31

"Envía a decir a todos los de la cautividad: Así dice Jehová de Semaías de Nehelam: Porque os profetizó Semaías, y yo no lo envié, y os hizo confiar en mentira:"

This verse carries deep theological significance. The original Hebrew/Greek text reveals layers of meaning that illuminate Gods character and purposes. The context within the broader narrative provides essential framework for understanding.

The historical backdrop adds richness to interpretation. Archaeological discoveries continue to confirm and illuminate the cultural setting in which these words were written.

32.texto_original: Por tanto, así dice Jehová: He aquí que yo voy a castigar a Semaías el nehelamita, y a su descendencia; no tendrá varón que more entre este pueblo, ni verá el bien que voy a hacer a mi pueblo, dice Jehová; porque contra Jehová ha hablado rebelión.
32.normal:
Devotional Study of Jeremías 29:32

"Por tanto, así dice Jehová: He aquí que yo voy a castigar a Semaías el nehelamita, y a su descendencia; no tendrá varón que more entre este pueblo, ni verá el bien que voy a hacer a mi pueblo, dice Jehová; porque contra Jehová ha hablado rebelión."

This verse carries deep theological significance. The original Hebrew/Greek text reveals layers of meaning that illuminate Gods character and purposes. The context within the broader narrative provides essential framework for understanding.

The historical backdrop adds richness to interpretation. Archaeological discoveries continue to confirm and illuminate the cultural setting in which these words were written.

For the modern reader, this verse offers both comfort and challenge. It invites us to trust in Gods sovereign plan while actively living out its principles in daily life.
